content:
Ryan, I’d do two things at once: shepherd my child during the reading, and pursue unity with my elder without surrendering what Scripture actually is.

With my 10-year-old during/after Psalm 19
- I’d lean over and quietly say, “This is a song. Songs use pictures to tell the truth. When it says the sun runs its course, that’s like when we say ‘sunrise’—it’s true in the way we normally speak, and it’s praising God.”  
- At lunch I’d unpack Psalm 19’s two halves: creation speaks about God’s glory (verses 1–6), and God’s Word speaks clearly about His will (verses 7–14). I’d show how the Bible can use poetic language and still tell the truth without teaching a science lesson.  
- I’d connect that to Genesis 1: “God really made everything by His word. Genesis 1 has beautiful structure and repetition, but it isn’t ‘just a poem.’ It’s true, authoritative Scripture. Faithful Christians notice its artistry and still believe it’s telling us real things God did: He created everything, ordered it, called it good, made people in His image, and set a pattern of work and rest.”  
- If my child asks about “how long” and “how,” I’d say, “Christians who love the Bible differ on some details. In our home we won’t pretend it’s merely poetry to avoid hard conversations. We’ll keep trusting that every part of God’s Word is true, and we’ll treat other believers with kindness when they see the timing differently.”

With my elder, for the sake of real unity
- I’d ask for a gracious, private conversation: “When you say ‘read Genesis 1 as poetic,’ do you mean ‘not historical,’ or do you mean ‘it has poetic features’? Are we binding consciences here?”  
- I’d appeal to our statement of faith and to sound hermeneutics: inerrancy means the Bible is true in all it affirms, according to its genre. Genesis 1 reads as exalted, structured narrative; Psalm 19 is clearly a psalm. We shouldn’t flatten either.  
- I’d suggest a better path to unity: clearly affirm what Genesis 1 certainly teaches (God as Creator of all, creation’s goodness and order, humanity in God’s image, the goodness of Sabbath rest), allow charitable diversity on timing frameworks among those who hold inerrancy, and teach our people how genre works.  
- I’d remind us both that “stumbling block” in 1 Corinthians 8 is about love in disputable matters, not about recasting Scripture’s intent. Unity is kept by humility and truth, not by papering over the text.

Bottom line on Sunday: I’ll let Psalm 19 do what God gave it to do—stir wonder—and I’ll use it to teach my child how poetry, everyday speech, and history all live faithfully under the authority of God’s inerrant Word. And I’ll keep pursuing peace with my elder without asking my child to call a song a science book, or a narrative a mere poem.
